children.text:
“Play is older than culture, for culture, however
inadequately defined, always presupposes human society, and animals have not
waited for man to teach them their playing”

Boardgames have existed for thousands of years, and there
are manifold ways that analogue games, particularly historical ones, have
influenced modern gaming culture and, as the quote from Huizinga suggests, play
is much, much older than the digital world, and, indeed, is one of the
fundamental conditions of humanity.

But what does it mean to be playful? How has our play
changed and adapted over time?

Throughout this project, you will see different
examples of boardgames found in the archaeological record. Some will feel very
familiar to you, and some might barely seem like boardgames at all. In each
case, these games were played by people just like us if not for a quirk of
temporality and geography. We invite you to step back and see what playing in
the past was like, and why people still find these games important today.

“Play is older than culture, for culture, however
inadequately defined, always presupposes human society, and animals have not
waited for man to teach them their playing” –
